prospects.thesis: GitLab's DevSecOps platform is being repositioned as an AI-native software delivery suite with Duo Enterprise driving seat expansion and higher ARPU. At 5.6x P/S with +29.3% one-month momentum and bullish institutional flows, it offers a rare combination of reasonable valuation and accelerating AI monetization. Entry now captures momentum ahead of the next earnings print where Duo attach rates should surprise.

Datadog is a direct beneficiary of AI workload observability as enterprises scale LLM deployments, with LLM Observability and Bits AI products driving new logo wins. Bullish options flow, bullish institutional positioning, and +17.8% one-month move confirm smart money accumulation. The 25.3x P/S is rich but justified by re-accelerating usage growth on AI-native customers.

Microsoft remains the best-in-class large-cap AI monetization story via Azure OpenAI, Copilot enterprise attach, and Fabric data platform, with bullish options and institutional signals confirming conviction. At 9.3x P/S for a $2.97T franchise, it provides ballast for the AI theme with lower drawdown risk than pure-play SaaS. FY26 guidance and Azure growth reacceleration are the near-term catalysts.

AeroVironment is the pure-play beneficiary of the drone/loitering munitions procurement cycle, with Switchblade and Puma demand accelerating from Ukraine, Taiwan, and DoD Replicator Initiative funding. The -2.4% one-month pullback offers an attractive entry versus peers, while bullish options and institutional flows suggest smart money is positioning ahead of the next contract catalyst. Small-cap defense with tactical drone tailwinds is under-owned versus primes.

L3Harris offers exposure to C5ISR, tactical communications, and space/missile systems via the Aerojet Rocketdyne integration, aligning with FY26 defense budget priorities on munitions replenishment and space. The -1.9% one-month dip combined with bullish options positioning and institutional accumulation suggests a base is forming ahead of earnings. At 4.1x P/S it's reasonably valued versus primes with better growth mix.

market_pulse.rotation_analysis: Big money is aggressively loading up on Energy, Communication Services, Consumer Discretionary, and Healthcare — the Energy bid is particularly loud and lines up with the administration's "drill baby drill" posture, looser permitting, and the geopolitical premium from ongoing Middle East and Russia sanctions chess, while the Comm Services and Discretionary buying suggests smart money thinks the consumer isn't rolling over yet and that the AI ad-spend cycle at Meta and Google still has legs. The Healthcare bullishness is the sneaky one — it's likely a bet that the worst of the drug pricing and tariff rhetoric is priced in, and that any softening of Most-Favored-Nation pricing threats becomes a relief rally. On the exit side, the bearish flow in Financials, Industrials, and Materials is a giant red flag: those are the classic "real economy" sectors, and when combined with widening credit spreads and heavy hedging in investment-grade bonds (LQD), it screams that institutions are quietly bracing for tariff blowback, slower capex, and margin pressure from a manufacturing slowdown that the tariff regime is starting to bite into. The Tech bearishness is the contradiction worth staring at — the Nasdaq keeps making highs but the options market is betting big on a pullback, which usually means hedge funds are protecting gains rather than calling a crash, especially with antitrust cases and the AI capex-vs-revenue debate looming. Historically, this rotation pattern — defensive-yet-selective bullishness plus credit stress — precedes a choppy 4-8 weeks where the index masks real damage underneath as leadership narrows. The non-obvious tell: when credit spreads widen while equities hold up and everyone's hedging the cyclicals, the market isn't pricing recession
